mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the life and career of Muhammad (B.1.b), the early Muslim expansion under the Patriarchal Caliphs (B.1.c), Abu Bakr and Umar's campaigns (B.1.i), and the caliphate of Uthman and Ali including the origins of Shi'ism (B.1.i.ii). Foundational to the entire section's subject.

Ali ibn Abi Talib is central to B.1.i.ii, which covers his caliphate (656–661), civil unrest, and the origins of Shi'ism; he also connects to B.1.b (Muhammad's inner circle), B.1.c (Patriarchal Caliphs), and B.2.a (his rivalry with Mu'awiyah that enabled the Umayyad caliphate).

Covers the origins of Shi'ism in the disputes over Ali's succession (B.1.i.ii), opposition to the Umayyads (B.2.a), and the Fatimid caliphate as a Shi'ite state (C.2.b) and the Buyid dynasty's Shi'ism and Iranian revival (C.2.e).

The Quran is the primary product of Muhammad's prophethood (B.1.b) and was compiled into standardized written form under Caliph Uthman around 650 CE (B.1.i.ii), making it directly relevant to the section's coverage of the rise of Islam and the early caliphate.

Covers the cultural life of the Abbasid caliphate in Baghdad and Cordoba (C.1.d, C.1.i.ii), including mathematics, philosophy, medicine, and the arts driven by Abbasid patronage; also addresses the cultural splendour of Umayyad Spain (C.2.a, C.2.i.iii).

Avicenna (c.980–1037) was active in the Samanid and Buyid cultural spheres, directly addressed in C.2.d (Samanid patronage of art and learning) and C.2.e (Buyid dynasty, Iranian revival); his philosophical and medical work exemplifies C.1.d and C.1.i (arts and sciences, theology and philosophy under the Abbasids).

Spain (Al-Andalus) is the direct geographic subject of C.2.a–C.2.i.iii, covering the Umayyad emirate and caliphate in Spain from 756 to 1031, including the conquest by Tariq ibn Ziyad, the splendour of Abd ar-Rahman III's caliphate, and social, economic, and cultural life in Muslim Spain.

Egypt was conquered by Umar I's forces around 641 CE (B.1.i) and later became the base of the Fatimid caliphate's conquest from Tunisia in 969 CE (C.2.b), making both paths substantive coverage areas.

Iran (the Sasanid Persian Empire) was conquered under Abu Bakr and Umar (B.1.i) and further subjugated under Uthman and Ali (B.1.i.ii); the Samanid dynasty of Khorasan (C.2.d) and Buyid dynasty in Iran and Iraq (C.2.e) are both covered in Iran's historical narrative.

Algeria encompasses the territories of the Rustamid kingdom in the central Maghrib (787–911) and the Aghlabid state in Tunisia/Algeria/Sicily (800–909), both explicitly listed under C.2.c as other North African and Syrian dynasties.

The spread of the Arabic language under the Umayyad caliphate is explicitly listed under B.2.i.ii (cultural life under the Umayyads: spread of Arabic language, literary revival); B.2.i covers the Arabization policy as part of caliphate administration.

The Byzantine Empire lost Syria, Palestine, Jordan, and Egypt to the Arab conquests under Umar I, directly covered in B.1.i and B.1.c (Muslim expansion outside Arabia, conquest of Byzantine territories).

Saudi Arabia occupies the Arabian Peninsula and is the birthplace of Islam, providing direct coverage of Arabia before Muhammad (B.1.a) and the rise of Islam from Mecca and Medina (B.1.b), including the custodianship of Islam's holiest sites.

The Silk Road trade network supported economic and social life under the Abbasids (C.1.c: manufactures and trade) and the Samanid dynasty of Khorasan played a key role in Central Asian trade along its routes (C.2.d).

C.2.i explicitly mentions the defeat of the Muslims near Poitiers by the Frank Charles Martel (732), and Charlemagne's grandfather's victory forms the political context for the limits of Umayyad expansion into Western Europe that frames the Umayyad emirate in Spain.

Post-classical history (c.500–1450) frames the Islamic Golden Age, Abbasid caliphate, and trans-civilizational trade networks as defining features of the period, providing broad historiographic and contextual coverage across paths A through C of this section.
